coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis for chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid sensing and logic gate operation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Huan
He, Li
Long, Yuwei
Li, Hongxi
Pan, Shuang
Liu, Hui
Hu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Herein, nitrogen, sulfur co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,SCDs) were synthesized by simple and facile microwave-assisted pyrolysis using ammonium citrate and cysteamine hydrochloride as precursors. The obtained N,SCDs exhibited outstanding photostability, excitation-wavelength independence, excellent fluorescence properties with fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) up to 54.8%. Significantly, the fluorescence of N,SCDs was effectively quenched by Cr(VI) based on inner filter effect (IFE). Subsequently, the fluorescence of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system successfully recovered with addition of ascorbic acid (AA) owing to redox reaction between Cr(VI) and AA. Therefore, N,SCDs could be employed as an efficient fluorescent “turn-off” probe for highly sensitive detection of Cr(VI), with a liner detection range from 0.35 to 126.0 μmol L−1 and a detection limit of 0.11 μmol L−1. Meanwhile, the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system could be used as an fluorescent “turn-on” sensor for detection of AA with a detection limit of 0.17 μmol L−1 and a liner detection range of 0.57–264.0 μmol L−1. Interestingly, a molecular logic gate was constructed based on the fluorescence recover of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system in the presence of AA. Most importantly, this fluorescent probe has been applied for determination of Cr(VI) and AA in environmental and biological fields with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen
Sulfur co-doped fluorescent carbon dots
Cr(VI)
Ascorbic acid
Inner filter effect
Logic gate operation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for Cr(VI) and AA Detection
Mechanism for Detecting Cr(VI) by N,SCDs
Sensitivity of N,SCDs-Cr(VI) for AA and Its Possible Mechanism
Detection of Cr(VI) in Water Samples
Detection of AA in Real Samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N,SCDs (inset: the diameter distribution of N,SCDs). (B) FTIR spectrum of as-prepared N,SCDs. (C) XRD pattern of N,SCDs. (D) UV absorption and FL excitation, emission spectra of N,SCDs. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of N,SCDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 380 nm.

(A) FL intensity of different excitation time for N,SCDs solutions. (B) FL intensity of N,SCDs solutions at different concentrations NaCl. (C) FL intensity of N,SCDs solutions under various temperatures. V N,SCDs = 0.2 mL, λ ex = 350 nm.

(A) The effect of pH for Cr(VI). (B) The effect of the volume of N,SCDs on the fluorescence quenching efficiency. (C) The optimization of the reaction time for Cr(VI). (D) The influence of reaction time for AA. V N,SCDs = 0.2 mL, C Cr(VI) = 7.8 × 10−5 mol L−1, C AA = 2.0 × 10−4 mol L−1. V BR = 1.0 mL, pH:7.0.

(A) FL emission spectra of N,SCDs in the presence of different concentration of Cr(VI). (B) Calibration curve for Cr(VI). V N,SCDs = 0.2 mL, a-i C Cr(VI): 0; 0.6; 1.8; 3.0; 4.2; 5.4; 6.6; 7.8; 9.0; 10.2; 11.4; 12.6 × 10−5 mol L−1, V BR = 1.0 mL, pH:7.0.

(A) Fluorescence excitation (Ex), emission (Em) spectrums of N,SCDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) (Abs). (B) Fluorescence decay of N,SCDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI). V N,SCDs = 0.2 mL, C Cr(VI) = 7.8 × 10−5 mol L−1, V BR = 1.0 mL, pH:7.0.

Normalized fluorescence of N,SCDs in the absence and presence of different metal ions or anions. V N,SCDs = 0.2 mL, C Cr(VI) = 1.26 × 10−4 mol L−1, the concentration of each foreign ions was 1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1, V BR = 1.0 mL, pH:7.0.

(A) FL restoration of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system upon the addition of different concentrations AA. (B) Calibration curve for AA. V N,SCDs = 0.2 mL, C Cr(VI) = 1.26 × 10−4 mol L−1, a-j C AA: 0; 0.8; 4.0; 7.2; 10.4; 13.6; 16.8; 20.0; 23.2; 26.4 × 10−5 mol L−1, V BR = 1.0 mL, pH:7.0.

Relatively fluorescence signal response to AA and other potential interference species in N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system. V N,SCDs = 0.2 mL, C Cr(VI) = 1.26 × 10−4 mol L−1, C AA = 2.64 × 10−4 mol L−1, the concentration of each foreign ions was 1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1.
Truth Table (A), symbol (B) of the logic gate operation.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of N,SCDs, as well as the Cr(VI) and AA detection based on N,SCDs.
Comparison of analytical methods for the detection of Cr(VI).

row.entry: Simple and sensitive, but the precision is lower
Low detection limit but sophisticated instruments are needed
Sensitive, selective yet it is difficult to obtain the functionalized electrode
Facile, selective, sensitive and available for common lab

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of analytical methods for the detection of AA.

row.entry: selective and sensitive, but complicated and tedious steps to fabricate functionalized electrodes
Sensitive, selective yet it takes time to prepare the thin films
Low detection limit but needs expensive instruments
Low-cost, simple, stable yet poor selectivity
Sensitive, selective, rapid, and no need for organic solvents

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND*: not detected. RSD: relative standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The result of determination of AA in real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND*: not detected. RSD: relative standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis for chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid sensing and logic gate operation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time analytical Chemistry (Southwest University)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, nitrogen, sulfur co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (N,SCDs) were synthesized by simple and facile microwave-assisted pyrolysis using ammonium citrate and cysteamine hydrochloride as precursors. The obtained N,SCDs exhibited outstanding photostability, excitation-wavelength independence, excellent fluorescence properties with fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) up to 54.8%. Significantly, the fluorescence of N,SCDs was effectively quenched by Cr(VI) based on inner filter effect (IFE). Subsequently, the fluorescence of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system successfully recovered with addition of ascorbic acid (AA) owing to redox reaction between Cr(VI) and AA. Therefore, N,SCDs could be employed as an efficient fluorescent “turn-off” probe for highly sensitive detection of Cr(VI), with a liner detection range from 0.35 to 126.0 μmol L−1 and a detection limit of 0.11 μmol L−1. Meanwhile, the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system could be used as an fluorescent “turn-on” sensor for detection of AA with a detection limit of 0.17 μmol L−1 and a liner detection range of 0.57–264.0 μmol L−1. Interestingly, a molecular logic gate was constructed based on the fluorescence recover of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system in the presence of AA. Most importantly, this fluorescent probe has been applied for determination of Cr(VI) and AA in environmental and biological fields with satisfactory results.

Scheme 1 Schematic illustration of the synthesis of N,SCDs, as well as the Cr(VI) and AA detection based on N,SCDs.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the attention for highly sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal ions has increased owing to growing environmental pollution. Among various heavy metals ions, chromium has been widely used in tanning, metallurgy, lumbering industry and unjustified utilization of chromium will lead to serious environment and soil bodies pollution [1]. Although chromium exists in oxidation states from −4 to +6, hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) and trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) are the primary states in environment [2]. Cr(III) is regarded as an essential trace element for maintaining normal human biological functions, which plays vital role in lipid and protein metabolism and insulin control [3]. In contrast, Cr(VI) is considered as a toxic species to the environment and human health due to its higher oxidation potential and detrimental effects on cell division, the toxicity of Cr(VI) is 500–1000 times higher than that of Cr(III) [4,5]. Given by its toxicity, the maximum level (50.0 μg/L) of Cr(VI) in drinking and ground water was limited by the World Health Organization (WHO) [6]. Up to now, a plethora of protocols have been explored for detection of Cr(VI), such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [7], electrochemistry [8], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [9], colorimetry [10]. Many of these methods are sensitive and selective, while they also suffer from costly, time-consuming, complicated pretreatment process and the operation of sophisticated instruments, which limit their wide applications. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop facile, rapid and economical detection methods for sensitive and selective determination of Cr(VI).

Ascorbic acid (AA) is a water-soluble vitamin known for its reductive characteristics, which is widespread in fruits, vegetables, animal viscera etc. [11]. The abnormal level of AA is closely related with many diseases, including scurvy, mental illness, diarrhea, cancer, and AIDS [12]. In addition, it also has been commonly employed as an antioxidant in foods, drinks, and cosmetic [13]. Extensive studies have shown that insufficient intake will lead to scurvy, gingival bleeding, skin petechiae, and so on, while excessive intake will result in urinary stone and stomach convulsion [14,15]. Therefore, the development of simple, rapid methods for determination of AA with high sensitivity and selectivity is essential to food quality and health care. To date, copious analytic methods for AA detection have been developed, including electrochemistry [16], colorimetry [17], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [18], chemiluminescence [19] and fluorescence spectroscopy [20–23]. Among these, fluorescence method is superior to others owing to its rapid response, simple operation, and convenience. Therefore, more facile, effective and sensitive methods for detection of AA are still desired.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials, have garnered tremendous attention because of their intriguing properties, such as excellent photostability, tunable fluorescence emission, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity and good water-solubility [24]. Due to these exceptional properties make them widely used in bioimaging [25], biosensing [26], medical diagnosis [27], photodynamic therapy [28], catalysis [29], and photovoltaic devices [30]. Although CDs have shown many outstanding performances, relatively low FLQY is still a challenge and need to be enhanced. In order to improve FLQY, the researchers have dedicated to dope different atoms into CDs. Because these atoms have different atomic orbits compared with carbon, thus forming a new surface state to trap excited electrons, which can increase density and enhance the FLQY [31]. For example, Zhang et al. synthesized fluorescent N-doped CDs using ammonium citrate and ethylenediamine as precursor with FLQY of 66.8% [32]. In addition, Xu et al. prepared sulfur-doped CDs with significant FLQY (67%), which indicated that sulfur doping achieved an effective improvement of FLQY [33]. Meanwhile, Zhao and co-worker synthesized N-S co-doped CDs with FLQY of 74.15% by using ammonium thiocyanate as the sulfur and nitrogen source [34]. In view of heteroatom doping can efficiently enhance FLQY, it is essential to develop rapid, convenient doping approaches to achieve high-performance fluorescent CDs.

In this work, we reported a simple and quick strategy for synthesizing fluorescent N,SCDs using ammonium citrate and cysteamine hydrochloride by microwave-assisted approach. The as-prepared N,SCDs exhibited good photostability, excitation-wavelength independence and high FLQY (54.8%). Significantly, the N,SCDs was developed for sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI) based on IFE, the quenching fluorescence could be successfully recovered when addition of AA. Based on the FL change, we constructed a facile chemical sensor for detection of Cr(VI) and AA synchronously. What's more, we have also demonstrated the practical feasibility of this approach for detection of Cr(VI) and AA in real samples. Moreover, the present study also provided a new insight to develop facile and sensitive biosensors.

The ammonium citrate was purchased from Jinshan Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Cysteamine hydrochloride was obtained from Aladdin reagent (Shanghai, Co., Ltd., China). Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) and ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Kelon Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). The BR buffer solutions of various pH were prepared by varying the ratio of the mixed acid (composed of 2.71 mL 85% H3PO4, 2.36 mL HAc and 2.47 g H3BO3) with 0.2 mol L−1 NaOH. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used throughout the whole experiments.

All fluorescence measurements were carried out using an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was recorded by using a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (FEI, USA) at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectrum was performed on FTIR-8400S FTIR spectrometer. The AXS-D8 X-Ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) was employed to record X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum. Fluorescence lifetime was obtained by an FL-TCSPC Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France) at room temperature. A PHS-3C pH meter (Shanghai Leici Instrument Company, Ltd., China) was adopted to measure pH values of solutions.

Photoluminescent N,SCDs were synthesized by the microwave-assisted pyrolysis of ammonium citrate in the presence of cysteamine hydrochloride. In a typical procedure, 0.3 g ammonium citrate and 0.2 g cysteamine hydrochloride were dissolved in 30.0 mL ultrapure water to form a transparent solution, then the mixture was heated in an ordinary household microwave oven for 4 min in high power mode. The large body of yellow N,SCDs powders were obtained directly and were redissolved in 30.0 mL ultrapure water. The obtained N,SCDs solution was subjected to dialysis (molecular weight cut off = 3500) for 24 h. Finally, the purified N,SCDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further characterization and use.

The fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of the N,SCDs was measured with quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 as reference, which quantum yield is 54% at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The FLQY was calculated by the following equation:
Φ u
=
Φ s
F u
A s
n u 2
F s
A u
n s 2
where Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, u represents the sample, s stands for the reference compound, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and F refers to the integrated fluorescence intensity. n represents the refractive index of the solvent (n
u and n
s are 1.33).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for Cr(VI) and AA Detection
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical run, 0.2 mL of N,SCDs solution was taken into 1.0 mL BR buffer solution, followed by addition of an appropriate concentration of Cr(VI) ions. The mixture was diluted to 10.0 mL with ultrapure water and stirred thoroughly. After incubation for 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded for the quantitative analysis of Cr(VI) at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

An assay of AA detection was carried out by following redox reaction. 1.0 mL pH 7.0 BR buffer, 0.63 mL, 2.0 mmol L−1 of Cr(VI) solution, the appropriate aliquot of AA solution and 0.2 mL of N,SCDs solution added into a 10.0 mL volumetric flask orderly and final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 10.0 mL with ultrapure water. After 10 min incubation at room temperature, the FL intensity of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI)-AA was measured with an excitation at 350 nm.

Herein, the fluorescent N,SCDs with a high quantum yield were synthesized using ammonium citrate and cysteamine hydrochloride by microwave-assisted pyrolysis process. Compared to other synthetic methods, microwave-assisted pyrolysis has been considered as a facile and high-efficiency method to synthesize CDs due to it time-saving, energy conservation, cost-effective, and convenient operation [35]. Microwave-assisted method, which provides intensive and homogeneous energy to reaction system, has been reported to be applied for preparation of heteroatom doping CDs [36]. The rapid and direct heating, which gets rid of influence of temperature gradients. Furthermore, microwave-assisted enhanced the fluorescence properties of N,SCDs may be attributed to the possibility of obtaining temperatures above the boiling point by working at increased pressure, resulted in the fast preparation of monodisperse, excitation-wavelength independence and highly quantum yield N,SCDs. [37]. In addition, the present synthesis method via microwave irradiation is simpler and more efficient. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the synthetic process just took 4 min, and the obtained N,SCDs exhibited intriguing fluorescence properties and FLQY up to 54.8%.

The morphology and sizes of the as-synthesized N,SCDs were shown in Fig. 1A, and the obtained N,SCDs were mainly distributed in the range of 1.69–6.08 nm with an average size of 3.58 nm. In order to investigate the surface functional groups of the N,SCDs, FTIR analysis was performed and the result was displayed in Fig. 1B. The absorption band at 3448 cm−1 was attributed to NH and/or OH groups. The peaks at 2926 cm−1 and 1701 cm−1 can be assigned to the CH stretching vibration and CO vibrations stretching, respectively. Two peaks centered at 1401 cm−1 and 1336 cm−1 were ascribed to the symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate groups on the surface of N,SCDs. The characteristic absorption peaks of CO and CS stretching vibrations were observed at 1153 cm−1. To further obtain a better insight of the formation of N,SCDs, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were explored. As illustrated in Fig. 1C, N,SCDs presented a broad diffraction peak centered around 24°, which was attributed to the highly disordered carbon.

In order to demonstrate optical properties, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N,SCDs were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 1D, a absorption band located at 342 nm was observed, which was assigned to the n-π* transition of the CO/CNH2, resulting in strong fluorescence emission. In addition, the fluorescence emission spectra of N,SCDs at different excitation wavelengths were recorded. As plotted in Fig. 1E, the emission wavelength of N,SCDs was fixed at 422 nm even excitation wavelength range of 300–380 nm, and the maximum excitation wavelength centered at 350 nm. The constant maximum emission wavelength indicated that the N,SCDs is homogenous.

The stability of N,SCDs was studied at different UV light exposure times, ionic strengths, and temperature. As shown in Fig. 2A, the as-prepared N,SCDs were irradiated by UV light (365 nm) for 60 min and results revealed that FL intensity remained almost unchanged, which indicated that N,SCDs possessed outstanding photostability. In addition, no significant change was observed in the FL intensity of N,SCDs with the concentration of NaCl increasing from 0 to 0.6 mol L−1 (Fig. 2B). Moreover, Fig. 2C plotted the influence of different temperatures on the FL intensity. It was clear that the FL intensity of N,SCDs kept stable when temperature increased from 5 °C to 45 °C, suggesting that temperature had a little effect on the N,SCDs solution. Therefore, all experiments performed at room temperature.

In order to obtain optical response to the fluorescence probe, the detection conditions such as pH, the volume of N,SCDs, reaction time were explored. As illustrated in Fig. 3A, the FL intensity of N,SCDs changed within pH range from 2 to 12, indicating that pH had significant effect on the FL intensity of the N,SCDs. With increment of pH, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (ΔF) gradually increased and reached maximal value at 8.0. When pH value was above 8.0, ΔF remained almost unchanged. Taking into account the alkaline conditions were not conducive to the redox reaction between AA and Cr(VI) [38]. Thus, pH 7.0 was used as the aqueous medium for Cr(VI) detection. Meanwhile, the influence of different volumes of N,SCDs on FL intensity was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B, ΔF gradually increased and kept stable at 0.2 mL. Therefore, 0.2 mL N,SCDs solution was selected at the present study. In addition, the effect of reaction time on the Cr(VI) detection was displayed in Fig. 3C. Only within 5 min, can the reaction system reached the quenching equilibrium, indicating that the quenching process is fairly fast. Hence, 5 min was identified to the incubation time for Cr(VI). Moreover, the FL restoration of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system induced by AA was sensitive to the incubation time. As plotted in Fig. 3D, the FL recovery value of N,SCDs tended to be constant after 10 min. Accordingly, the incubation time was chosen as 10 min for AA detection.

Under the optimized conditions, various concentrations of Cr(VI) were added to the N,SCDs solution and FL emission spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 4A, the FL intensity of N,SCDs was decreased gradually with increasing Cr(VI) concentration, which was ascribed to the IFE between N,SCDs and Cr(VI). Fig. 4B presented the relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentration of Cr(VI), a linear relationship can be expressed as ΔF = F
0 − F
1 = 146c + 139 with a good correlation coefficient (0.9977) in the linear range from 0.35 to 126.0 μmol L−1. Here, F
0 and F
1 represent the FL intensity of N,SCDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI). The detection limit for Cr(VI) was calculated to be 0.11 μmol L−1 based on 3σ/s (σ stands for the standard deviation, s represents the slope of the calibration curve). Compared to other detection method for Cr(VI), N,SCDs fluorescent probe exhibited a lower detection limit for Cr(VI) (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for Detecting Cr(VI) by N,SCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore fluorescence quenching mechanism of N,SCDs toward Cr(VI), UV–vis absorbance spectrum of Cr(VI) and excitation and emission spectra of N,SCDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5A, Cr(VI) exhibited absorption peaks at 256, 351 and 430 nm, respectively, the excitation spectrum of N,SCDs centered at 350 nm and the emission band of N,SCDs located in 422 nm. That's to say, the absorption peaks of Cr(VI) precisely overlapped with the excitation spectra and the emission spectra of N,SCDs. Consequently, a part of the emission light from N,SCDs could be absorbed by Cr(VI), the excitation light of N,SCDs could also be shielded by Cr(VI) partly. Thus, the Cr(VI) could successfully quench the fluorescence of N,SCDs, which was ascribed to the IFE takes place in an efficient way. To further verify the quenching mechanism of N,SCDs toward Cr(VI), the lifetimes of N,SCDs without and with Cr(VI) were measured and the results were illustrated in Fig. 5B. The average lifetime values of N,SCDs and N,SCDs-Cr(VI) were 7.34 and 7.04 ns, respectively. The slightly changes of lifetime indicated that the fluorescence quenching of N,SCDs by Cr(VI) was ascribed to IFE.

In order to evaluate selectivity for detection of Cr(VI), the fluorescence quenching efficiency in the presence of other common ions (Ag+, K+, Na+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Pd2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, ClO−, S2O3 2−, NO2 −) were investigated under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 , metal ions and anions did not cause changes in the relative fluorescence intensity (F
1 /F
0 ), a much higher degree of fluorescence quenching was observed for N,SCDs upon addition of Cr(VI), indicating that other common ions showed no obvious interference to Cr(VI). Here, F
0 and F
1 represented the fluorescence intensity of N,SCDs without and with different metal ions or anions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of N,SCDs-Cr(VI) for AA and Its Possible Mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
In view of redox reaction between Cr(VI) and AA, the N,SCD-Cr(VI) system could serve as a “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for detection of AA. For N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system, Cr(VI) could be reduced to Cr(III) by AA, resulting in the elimination of the IFE and recovery of N,SCDs fluorescence. The fluorescence of N,SCDs was quenched by Cr(VI) based on IFE, upon addition of AA, the “off” fluorescence of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system turned on. As illustrated in Fig. 7A, with the concentration of AA increased, the FL intensity of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) enhanced gradually. Fig. 7B displayed that the fluorescence recovering efficiency versus the concentration of AA exhibited a good linearity in the range from 0.57 μmol L−1 to 264.0 μmol L−1. The linear relationship was expressed as follow: ΔF = F
2 − F
1 = 60c + 130.16 (F
2 and F
1 referred to the FL intensity of N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system in the presence and absence of AA). The correlation coefficient was R
2 = 0.9983 with limit detection of 0.17 μmol L−1. This result was better than those in the reported literature (Table 2 ).

To evaluate the selectivity of AA detection, the potential interference foreign species, including glycine, alanine, leucine, tyrosine, tryptophan, serine, histidine, arginine, lysine, methionine, glutathione, L-cysteine, dopamine, glutathione, riboflavin, glucose, sucrose, were also explored. As presented in Fig. 8 , F
2 /F
1 showed obvious trend of increasing for N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system upon addition of AA, F
1 and F
2 standed for the fluorescence intensity of N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system without and with interference foreign species, respectively. The fluorescence of N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system had no obvious enhancement in the presence of other species, while AA could rapidly recover the fluorescence of N,SCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in Water Samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to valid the feasibility of this fluorescent probe, tap water, lake water, and Jialing river were utilized to evaluate the applicability and reliability of the proposed method. The tap water was taken from our lab without any pretreatment. The lake water and river water were obtained from the Southwest university campus and the Jialing river, respectively. The lake water and river water were boiled at first, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The determination results were shown in Table 3 . It was obvious that spiked recoveries were in the range of 95.3%–103.1% and the RSD was lower than 4.59%. As a consequence, the developed fluorescent probe was sensitive to Cr(VI). This results indicated that the present method has great potential for determination of Cr(VI) in water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in Real Samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applications of N,SCDs-Cr(VI) for AA detection, the orange juice and lemon juice were selected for investigation. All experiments were carried out by standard addition method and the results were illustrated in Table 4 . It is worth noting that quantitative spike recoveries for detection of AA in above real samples ranged from 98.0% to 102.8%, and relative standard deviation was less than 3.4%. Therefore, the N,SCDs-Cr(VI)-based sensor could be developed as a promising platform for sensitive and selective detection of AA in real samples.

Based on significant fluorescence recovery of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system upon addition of AA, a molecular logic gate was developed. In this logic gate, Cr(VI) and AA were served as inputs and fluorescence signal acted as output. For inputs, the presence of Cr(VI) or AA were considered to be the “1” and their absence as “0”. For outputs, fluorescence recovery and non-fluorescence recovery intensity were defined as “1” and “0”, respectively. With no input (0, 0), and Cr(1,0) or AA (0,1) input alone, the output was “0”. Inversely, the recovered fluorescence emission of N,SCDs-Cr(VI) in the presence of AA revealed that output was “1”. Therefore, a molecular logic gate was constructed where the truth Table (A) and symbol (B) were illustrated in Fig. 9 .

In summary, a facile, simple and one-pot microwave method for the synthesis of fluorescent N,SCDs was developed using ammonium citrate and cysteamine hydrochloride as precursors. The as-prepared N,SCDs exhibited outstanding photostability, excitation-wavelength independence, excellent fluorescence properties with FLQY as high as 54.8%. Significantly, the fluorescence of N,SCDs could be sensitively and selectively quenched by Cr(VI) based on IFE. Meanwhile, an obvious fluorescence recover was observed when AA was injected into N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system. This occurred owing to AA has high reductibility toward Cr(VI), resulting in the elimination of the IFE and recovering the fluorescence of N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system. As for Cr(VI), a good linearity was attained over the concentration range from 0.35 to 126.0 μmol L−1 with a detection limit of 0.11 μmol L−1. As for AA, a detection limit of 0.17 μmol L−1 were achieved with the linear range of 0.57–264.0 μmol L−1. Importantly, this “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor has been successfully employed to detect Cr(VI) and AA in real samples with satisfactory results. In addition, a molecular logic gate was developed based on the significant fluorescence recover of the N,SCDs-Cr(VI) system in the presence of AA. On the basis of exceptional performance, the present strategy may offered a new method for developing convenient, high sensitive and selective sensors in environmental and biological sensing applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support for this study by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 21705132) and the Special Fund of Chongqing Key Laboratory (CSTC).
